Dog Weights R Assignment

Alyssah Mack

Step 1

Read dog weights file into R studio

dogData <- read.csv("dog_weights.csv")

Make a boxplot comparing the weights of beagles, pitbulls, and dalmatians. Assign each a color, add a title, y-axis label, and legend.

boxplot(dog_weight ~ dog_type, data=dogData, 
    xlab="Dog Type", 
    ylab="Dog Weight (lbs)", 
    col=c("lightpink", "lightgreen", "lightblue"),
    main="Weights of Different Dog Breeds",
    cex.lab=0.75,
    cex.main=1)

legend(0.5, 120, c("Beagle", "Pitbull", "Dalmatian"), 
       fill = c("lightpink","lightblue", "lightgreen"),
       cex=0.7)

Step 2

Transform the raw data values for pitbull weights to z-scores and make a plot with two panels.

library(dplyr)

pitbull <- filter(dogData, dog_type=="pitbull")
z_scores <- scale(pitbull$dog_weight)

par(mfrow=c(1,2))
hist((pitbull$dog_weight), main="Raw Pitbull Weights", xlab= "Weight (lbs)",
  col="cornflowerblue")
hist((z_scores), main="Z-score Pitbull Weights", xlab="z-score",
     col="mediumpurple")

Step 3

Perform a one sample t-test to test whether the weight of beagles is significantly different from 21 lbs.

beagle <- filter(dogData, dog_type=="beagle")

t.test(beagle$dog_weight, mu=21)

    One Sample t-test

data:  beagle$dog_weight
t = -1.681, df = 177, p-value = 0.09453
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 21
95 percent confidence interval:
 18.63426 21.18934
sample estimates:
mean of x 
  19.9118

Perform a two sample t-test to test whether the weight of pitbulls is significantly different from the weight of dalmatians.

dalmatian <- filter(dogData, dog_type=="dalmatian")

t.test(dalmatian$dog_weight, pitbull$dog_weight)

    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  dalmatian$dog_weight and pitbull$dog_weight
t = 13.455, df = 335.74, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 17.21068 23.10433
sample estimates:
mean of x mean of y 
 70.07222  49.91472

Interpretation

Dalmatians, on average, weigh the most compared to beagles and pitbulls, with beagles weighing the least. Z-scores shows how many standard deviations a value is away from the mean of the data set. Based on the z-score histogram of the pitbull weights, majority of the data lies within 1 standard deviation of the mean. T-tests determine if there are significant differences between the means of two groups, whether they are of unrelated groups or compared to theoretical means. The higher the result of the t-test, the more difference there is between the means being compared. Knowing this, the mean beagle weight is not significantly different from 21 lbs, but the mean weights of dalmatians and pitbulls are significantly different.
